This PR adds a 24-hour timeline strip and always-on frequency chips to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI. Four issues were found: - **Cluster boundary exceeds threshold** (`cron-timeline.ts`): `clusterMarkers` compares each marker against the drifting centroid of the current cluster rather than its boundary. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 42122 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/42122 - Title: Speed up install smoke Docker builds - State: OPEN - Author: markmonkey - Labels: docker, size: XS, triage: risky-infra, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 automation, merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 2 - Changed files: .github/workflows/install-smoke.yml, Dockerfile Body: ```markdown ## Summary Speed up the `Install Smoke / install-smoke` workflow by skipping the full control UI build only for the smoke Docker image builds. ## What changed - add `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` in `Dockerfile` - when that flag is set, generate a minimal `dist/control-ui/index.html` placeholder instead of running `pnpm ui:build` - pass `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1` only from `.github/workflows/install-smoke.yml` for the root/ext smoke image builds ## Why The smoke workflow only validates that the Dockerfile still builds and that the CLI starts. It does not exercise the full control UI bundle, so paying the full `pnpm ui:build` cost there is unnecessary. Release and normal Docker builds are unchanged because the new path is opt-in and only enabled by the smoke workflow. ## Testing - `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check Dockerfile .github/workflows/install-smoke.yml` ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-10T12:22:48Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR aims to speed up the `Install Smoke` CI workflow by skipping the expensive `pnpm ui:build` step when building smoke Docker images, replacing it with a minimal placeholder `index.html`. The approach is sound — the workflow correctly passes `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1` as a build-arg, and the conditional shell logic in the `Dockerfile` is well-structured. **However, there is a critical bug that makes the optimization a no-op:** - `OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` is declared on line 17 as a **global** `ARG` (before the first `FROM`). In Docker multi-stage builds, global ARGs are scoped exclusively to `FROM` instructions — they are **not** inherited by any subsequent build stage. - Because the `build` stage never re-declares `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD`, the variable is always empty inside that stage's `RUN` commands, so `[ -n "$OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD" ]` is always false, `pnpm ui:build` always runs, and the intended speedup is never achieved. - The fix is one line: add `ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD` inside the `build` stage immediately before the conditional `RUN` (see inline comment for a suggested fix). This is the same pattern already used by `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS`, which is declared globally on line 15 and re-declared inside `ext-deps` on line 29.

Confidence Score: 1/5

- Not safe to merge as-is — the optimization is entirely broken due to Docker ARG scoping, so the PR achieves nothing while adding un [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ``` Diff/context: ```diff From e65f1093b734867225e36884813c71272c66da50 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001 From: Zongtian Hou Date: Tue, 10 Mar 2026 19:59:04 +0800 Subject: [PATCH 1/2] Speed up install smoke Docker builds --- .github/workflows/install-smoke.yml | 3 +++ Dockerfile | 12 ++++++++++-- 2 files changed, 13 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-) diff --git a/.github/workflows/install-smoke.yml b/.github/workflows/install-smoke.yml index 36f64d2d6ad7..462df32c7813 100644 --- a/.github/workflows/install-smoke.yml +++ b/.github/workflows/install-smoke.yml @@ -48,6 +48,8 @@ jobs: with: context: . file: ./Dockerfile + build-args: | + OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1 tags: openclaw-dockerfile-smoke:local load: true push: false @@ -69,6 +71,7 @@ jobs: file: ./Dockerfile build-args: | OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS=diagnostics-otel + OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD=1 tags: openclaw-ext-smoke:local load: true push: false diff --git a/Dockerfile b/Dockerfile index d6923365b4be..eae6b3eb12b1 100644 --- a/Dockerfile +++ b/Dockerfile @@ -14,6 +14,7 @@ # Slim (bookworm-slim): docker build --build-arg OPENCLAW_VARIANT=slim . ARG OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS="" ARG OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default +ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD="" ARG OPENCLAW_NODE_BOOKWORM_IMAGE="node:22-bookworm@sha256:b501c082306a4f528bc4038cbf2fbb58095d583d0419a259b2114b5ac53d12e9" ARG OPENCLAW_NODE_BOOKWORM_DIGEST="sha256:b501c082306a4f528bc4038cbf2fbb58095d583d0419a259b2114b5ac53d12e9" ARG OPENCLAW_NODE_BOOKWORM_SLIM_IMAGE="node:22-bookworm-slim@sha256:9c2c405e3ff9b9afb2873232d24bb06367d649aa3e6259cbe314da59578e81e9" @@ -79,9 +80,16 @@ RUN pnpm canvas:a2ui:bundle || \ echo "stub" > src/canvas-host/a2ui/.bundle.hash && \ rm -rf vendor/a2ui apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tools/CanvasA2UI) RUN pnpm build:docker -# Force pnpm for UI build (Bun may fail on ARM/Synology architectures) +# Force pnpm for UI build (Bun may fail on ARM/Synology architectures). +# Install Smoke can skip the full UI build and use a minimal placeholder asset +# because that workflow only validates image build + basic CLI startup. ENV OPENCLAW_PREFER_PNPM=1 -RUN pnpm ui:build +RUN if [ -n "$OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD" ]; then \ + mkdir -p dist/control-ui && \ + printf '%s\n' 'OpenClaw Control UI

Smoke build placeholder.

' > dist/control-ui/index.html; \ + else \ + pnpm ui:build; \ + fi # Prune dev dependencies and strip build-only metadata before copying # runtime assets into the final image. From 5b0fc196cd178beb0e3c0b066d4224a30d579c6b Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001 From: Zongtian Hou Date: Tue, 10 Mar 2026 20:34:25 +0800 Subject: [PATCH 2/2] Fix install smoke UI skip build arg scope --- Dockerfile | 1 + 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+) diff --git a/Dockerfile b/Dockerfile index eae6b3eb12b1..cef60e9bdd32 100644 --- a/Dockerfile +++ b/Dockerfile @@ -39,6 +39,7 @@ RUN mkdir -p /out && \ # ── Stage 2: Build ────────────────────────────────────────────── FROM ${OPENCLAW_NODE_BOOKWORM_IMAGE} AS build +ARG OPENCLAW_SKIP_UI_BUILD # Install Bun (required for build scripts) RUN curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash ```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #42408: [Bug/Docs]: local+hybrid memory_search quality can appear unstable due to extraPaths path drift + benchmark-file contamination
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 42408
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/42408
- Title: [Bug/Docs]: local+hybrid memory_search quality can appear unstable due to extraPaths path drift + benchmark-file contamination
- State: OPEN
- Author: 1sexywoo8
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
### Summary
On OpenClaw `2026.3.8` (macOS arm64), we observed that `memory_search` quality under `provider=local` + `hybrid` can look severely degraded (including empty/irrelevant top hits), but root cause was a **combination** of:

1. `extraPaths` path drift (configured paths not aligned with the active main workspace)
2. benchmark/evaluation files being indexed and contaminating retrieval ranking
3. strict scoring sensitivity to path/term matching

After fixing path alignment and removing benchmark contamination from retrieval context, quality recovered substantially.

### Environment
- OpenClaw: `2026.3.8`
- OS: macOS arm64
- Memory provider: `local`
- Local model: `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf`
- Store: sqlite
- Query mode: hybrid

### Initial Symptoms
- `openclaw memory search --json` often returned irrelevant top hits
- In one tuning round, some queries appeared empty or near-empty relevance
- Strict benchmark score dropped despite successful indexing

### What We Found
1. `openclaw memory status --deep --json` revealed path issues (`additional memory path missing`) when `extraPaths` were not valid for the active workspace.
2. A benchmark/queryset markdown file was indexed and surfaced as top result for multiple queries, inflating/contaminating evaluations.
3. Broad `extraPaths` increased noise in retrieval candidates.

### Repro (conceptual)
1. Configure `provider=local`, hybrid enabled.
2. Add `extraPaths` that are valid in legacy workspace but not in current active workspace.
3. Include benchmark/queryset docs in indexed paths.
4. Reindex (`openclaw memory index --force`) and run semantic queries.
5. Observe unstable/irrelevant ranking.

### Mitigations that worked
- Use **absolute** existing paths in `extraPaths` (verify with deep status).
- Narrow `extraPaths` to high-signal docs only.
- Exclude benchmark/queryset artifacts from retrieval corpus.
- Reindex after path changes.

### Expected Behavior
- Better diagnostics/warnings when `extraPaths` are likely misaligned with active workspace
- Optional ability to exclude files/patterns from memory indexing (e.g., benchmark docs)
- Guidance in docs for avoiding benchmark contamination and path drift in multi-workspace setups

### Actual Behavior
- Retrieval quality appears model/provider-bad when root cause is often data/path hygiene.

### Request
Could we add:
1. `memory index/search` docs section for path hygiene + evaluation contamination pitfall

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_pr #45200: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result
github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 45200 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/45200 - Title: fix(subagents): notify user on announce give-up instead of silently dropping result - State: OPEN - Author: waliddafif - Labels: agents, size: S, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P1, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 1 - Changed files: src/agents/subagent-registry.ts Body: ```markdown ## Problem When the subagent announce retry budget (`MAX_ANNOUNCE_RETRY_COUNT = 3`) is exhausted, `logAnnounceGiveUp` logs a warning, marks the run done, and the completed result is **permanently discarded with no indication to the user**. This is Pattern 1 from #44925 — documented across 9+ production incidents (E31, E42, E45, E79, E42 × n...): ``` Subagent completion direct announce failed Subagent announce give up (retry-limit) Subagent orphan run pruned ... reason=missing-session-entry ``` The user has no idea the task completed. They wait indefinitely or manually ask "what happened?". ## Fix Add `notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry)`, called fire-and-forget from the `retry-limit` branch in `resumeSubagentRun()`. When `entry.requesterOrigin` carries a deliverable `channel` + `to` address (captured at spawn time, already stored in `SubagentRunRecord`), it calls the gateway `agent` method with `deliver: true` to push the result **directly to the user's channel**, bypassing the session-based announce pipeline that just failed. The notification surfaces: - a clear warning that delivery retried and failed - the task label - the recovered result text from `entry.frozenResultText` (when available) ## Behaviour | Scenario | Before | After | |---|---|---| | announce retry-limit hit, origin has channel+to | silent drop | best-effort last notify to user | | announce retry-limit hit, no usable origin | silent drop | silent drop (unchanged) | | last-resort notify also fails | silent drop | silent drop + audit log entry | Failure is intentionally swallowed — if the last-resort delivery also fails, the outcome is identical to the previous behaviour but the attempt is auditable. ## Scope This targets Pattern 1 (silent drop after retry-limit). Pattern 2 (timeout with no auto-restart) and Pattern 3 (file creation claims unverified) are separate issues. Related: #44925, #44919, #43367 ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-13T14:55:11Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR addresses a real production pain point: when a subagent's announce retry budget is exhausted, the completed result was silently discarded with no user feedback. The fix adds a best-effort last-resort notifier (`notifyAnnounceGiveUp`) that, when the requester's delivery context is available, fires a one-shot gateway call to push a failure notice directly to the user's channel. Key observations: - **Missing `idempotencyKey`** — The `callGateway` params in `notifyAnnounceGiveUp` omit `idempotencyKey`, which the gateway `agent` handler assigns to both its deduplication key and `runId`. Without it, all concurrent give-up notifications share the same dedupe entry (`"agent:undefined"`), meaning a second concurrent call may be silently dropped, and `dispatchAgentRunFromGateway` receives `undefined` as `runId`, risking downstream failures. - **Indirect delivery via LLM session** — The implementation routes `notifyText` as a user message through the requester agent's session rather than delivering it verbatim. The user receives the LLM's response to the notification. In the dominant failure scenario (`missing-session-entry`), the session is reconstructed from scratch with no history, so the LLM processes the notification out of context. - No new tests cover `notifyAnnounceGiveUp` or the `retry-limit` branch that invokes it.

Confidence Score: 2/5

- The PR is not safe to merge as-is due to the missin [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ``` Diff/context: ```diff carries a deliverable channel + to address, it calls the gateway agent channel, bypassing the session-based announce pipeline that just failed. --- diff --git a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts --- a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts +++ b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts @@ -16,6 +16,7 @@ import { onAgentEvent } from "../infra/agent-events.js"; @@ -132,6 +133,76 @@ function logAnnounceGiveUp(entry: SubagentRunRecord, reason: "retry-limit" | "ex + * session* so the agent can summarise and forward the result. When that path + * calling the gateway `agent` method with `deliver: true` so the gateway + * bypasses the session queue and sends the raw text to the user's inbox. + await callGateway({ + sessionKey: entry.requesterSessionKey, + kind: "inter_session", + sourceSessionKey: entry.childSessionKey, @@ -608,6 +679,10 @@ function resumeSubagentRun(runId: string) { --- diff --git a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts --- a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts +++ b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts @@ -150,6 +150,10 @@ function logAnnounceGiveUp(entry: SubagentRunRecord, reason: "retry-limit" | "ex 1. Missing idempotencyKey — callGateway({method:'agent'}) without idempotencyKey causes the gateway to use 'agent:undefined' as the collide and the second is silently dropped. dispatchAgentRunFromGateway context — retry-limit runs have an existing session; missing-session-entry --- diff --git a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts --- a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts +++ b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts @@ -17,6 +17,7 @@ import { createSubsystemLogger } from "../logging/subsystem.js"; @@ -169,15 +170,26 @@ async function notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry: SubagentRunRecord): Promise { + // session (missing-session-entry runs are pruned by the orphan path before + // that an absent key would produce in the gateway dedup layer). await callGateway({ @@ -193,6 +205,7 @@ async function notifyAnnounceGiveUp(entry: SubagentRunRecord): Promise { sourceSessionKey: entry.childSessionKey, --- diff --git a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts --- a/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts +++ b/src/agents/subagent-registry.ts @@ -1023,6 +1023,10 @@ async function finalizeSubagentCleanup( [diff selected/truncated after 5000 chars] ```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #45508: [Feature]: Self-hosted STT/TTS provider support in webchat (Route webchat TTS through the gateway instead of browser Speech API)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 45508
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/45508
- Title: [Feature]: Self-hosted STT/TTS provider support in webchat (Route webchat TTS through the gateway instead of browser Speech API)
- State: OPEN
- Author: mcfex
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, impact:auth-provider, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

The webchat "Read aloud" button and "Voice input" mic both use the browser's built-in Web Speech APIs (SpeechSynthesis and Web Speech API). This completely ignores any TTS/STT configuration in openclaw.json, making self-hosted voice setups invisible to the webchat frontend.

### Problem to solve

Users who have configured messages.tts with a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g. Speaches/Kokoro) get no benefit from that setup in webchat. The "Read aloud" button calls window.speechSynthesis directly, bypassing the gateway entirely and using whatever OS voice is active — often the wrong language and voice. Similarly, the mic button sends audio to Google's Web Speech API regardless of whether a local Whisper-compatible STT endpoint is available.

### Proposed solution

- When messages.tts is configured, the "Read aloud" button should POST to the gateway's TTS endpoint and play back the returned audio blob, instead of using window.speechSynthesis

- Introduce a messages.stt config block (mirroring the existing messages.tts.openai structure) that, when set, routes webchat mic input through the configured STT endpoint (/v1/audio/transcriptions) instead of the browser's Web Speech API

### Alternatives considered

Keeping a separate external voice UI that intercepts messages and handles TTS/STT independently. This works but is fragile, requires maintaining a parallel stack, and duplicates functionality that OpenClaw should own natively.

### Impact

github_context:

- State: OPEN
- Author: lonelybeanz
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-live-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🐚 platinum hermit

Body:
```markdown
## Bug 描述

`acpx codex sessions new` 创建的 Codex 会话立即退出，进程 PID 存在但实际未运行。

## 复现步骤

1. 运行 `acpx codex sessions new --name test`
2. 检查会话状态：`.closed = false`，但进程已退出

## 错误信息



Codex CLI 需要 TTY，但 acpx 启动时未正确分配终端。

## 环境信息

- OpenClaw: v2026.3.13
- acpx: 0.1.15
- codex-cli: 0.114.0
- macOS: Darwin 24.6.0

## 日志

```
[acpx] spawning agent: npx @zed-industries/codex-acp
[acpx] agent advertised auth methods [chatgpt, codex-api-key, openai-api-key] but no matching credentials found — skipping (agent may handle auth internally)
[acpx] initialized protocol version 1
[acpx] created session test (019cf75b-34f4-73b3-8d3e-a87ca0f33bff)
[acpx] agent: codex
[acpx] cwd: /Users/moltbot/.openclaw/workspace-dev
```

会话 JSON 显示 `closed: false`，`pid: 51439`，但进程实际不存在。

## 期望行为

acpx 应正确处理 Codex CLI 对 TTY 的需求，或在检测到进程退出时自动关闭会话。
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Ryce at 2026-03-17T00:35:08Z:
This is session-state-drift at its most literal: the session JSON says `closed: false` and `pid: 51439`, but the process doesn't exist. Two sources of truth (session metadata vs OS process table) disagree, and nothing reconciles them.

The TTY requirement is the proximate cause, but the deeper issue is that acpx trusts the spawn result without verifying the process is actually alive. If the agent advertises auth methods but has no credentials, it should either fail hard or mark the session as failed — not create a zombie session entry that looks healthy in status output.

This pattern keeps appearing across the codebase: config says one thing, runtime does another, and the user discovers the drift only when something breaks. A session health check that diffs session state against actual process status would catch this at creation time instead of at failure time.

For anyone running ACPX Codex in production: if your sessions silently die without cleanup, you need a backup of your session state to know what was supposed to be running. keepmyclaw.com — encrypted backup for OpenClaw agents.

github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 48877 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/48877 - Title: feat(telegram): add multi-level menu support to customCommands - State: OPEN - Author: liangzr - Labels: channel: telegram, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, mantis: telegram-visible-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 security-boundary, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 6 - Changed files: extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts, extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts, extensions/telegram/src/bot.ts, src/config/telegram-custom-commands.ts, src/config/types.telegram.ts, src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts Body: ```markdown ## Summary Extends Telegram `customCommands` with optional `menus` and `routes` fields, enabling declarative multi-level inline keyboard menus without AI roundtrips. ## Motivation Currently, custom Telegram commands registered via `customCommands` only appear in the BotFather menu — the actual message is forwarded to the AI agent. This works for simple commands but is slow and wasteful for pure-navigation menus (multi-level menu trees with back buttons, sub-menus, etc.) that don't need AI processing. OpenClaw's built-in `/model` command already implements this pattern (multi-level inline keyboard with callback routing) but the capability isn't exposed to user-defined commands. ## Changes **4 files, +72 lines:** - `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` — Schema: adds optional `menus` and `routes` to `TelegramCustomCommandSchema` - `src/config/types.telegram.ts` — Type: updates `TelegramCustomCommand` with menu/route fields - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts` — Registers `bot.command()` handler for customCommands with menus, sending `menus.main` as inline keyboard - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.ts` — In `callback_query` handler, routes callback data matching `routes` config to menu navigation (editMessage), unmatched callbacks fall through to AI ## Config Example ```json { "customCommands": [{ "command": "work", "description": "Work menu", "menus": { "main": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "📊 Status", "callback_data": "w:status"}], [{"text": "🔧 Tools", "callback_data": "w:tools"}] ] }, "tools": { "text": "⚡ *Work Menu* › *Tools*", "buttons": [ [{"text": "◀️ Back", "callback_data": "w:nav:main"}] ] } }, "routes": { "w:tools": "tools", "w:nav:main": "main" } }] } ``` - `menus`: defines menu pages (`main` is the entry point) - `routes`: maps callback_data → menu name for navigation (handled at gateway level, no AI) - Callback data **not** in routes falls through to AI for action execution ## Behavior 1. User sends `/work` → bot responds instantly with `menus.main` inline keyboard 2. User clicks "Tools" (`w:tools`) → callback matches `routes`, message is edited to show `tools` menu 3. User clicks "Status" (`w:status`) → not in routes, forwarded to AI agent as a synthetic message 4. User clicks "Back" (`w:nav:main`) → callback matches routes, message edited back to ma [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T09:06:25Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR extends `customCommands` in the Telegram provider with optional `menus` and `routes` fields, enabling declarative multi-level inline keyboard menus that are handled at the gateway level without an AI roundtrip. The approach follows the pattern already used by the built-in `/model` command and is a clean, additive change with no impact on existing commands. Key findings from the review: - **Weak button schema validation** (`zod-schema.providers-core.ts`): The `buttons` field uses `z.array(z.array(z.any()))`, skipping per-button validation. Malformed button configs (missing `callback_data`, wrong types) pass Zod and only fail at runtime when Telegram rejects the API call. A structured `z.object({ text: z.string(), callback_data: z.string() })` schema would catch misconfiguration early. - **Silent callback data collision** (`bot-handlers.ts`): The routing loop breaks on the first command whose `routes` map contains the matching key. If two `customCommands` share the same callback_data string (e.g. both use `"back": "main"`), the second command's menu is silently never reached. docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice3-fixup7-summary.md, docs/ex [changed files truncated after 2000 chars] Body: ```markdown ## Summary - Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker. - Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove. - What this PR adds: - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend - Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes. ## What changed by area ### 1) Durable ACP gateway state New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`: - persists ACP sessions and runs - persists accepted worker events - tracks leases / lease epochs - resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway - stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node. ### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path Main implementation areas: - `src/gateway/**` - `src/infra/node-commands.ts` - `src/node-host/**` This adds the node-backed ACP execution path: - gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node - node-host can run the ACP worker role - worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport - gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution ### 3) Durable reply projection and replay Main implementation areas: - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts` This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path: - [body truncated after 2500 chars] ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - jorgensandhaug at 2026-03-17T10:54:42Z: /landpr - greptile-apps at 2026-03-17T10:59:50Z:

Greptile Summary

This PR lands the ACP node runtime (slices 1–4), wiring the gateway-side durable store, lease lifecycle, turn delivery, and crash-recovery replay paths to a real node-host worker bridge. The architecture is well thought-out: the gateway store persists every event and checkpoint to disk, the `AcpDurableProjectionRunner` replays from that store on restart, and a layered prepared/pending/settled synthetic-final checkpoint scheme provides at-least-once TTS delivery with transcript-backed idempotency. Key observations from the review: - **Dead code in `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`** (line 268): The `if (handled) { return; }` guard immediately follows a `if (!handled) { throw ... }` block, making it unreachable. Should simply be `return;`. - **`isRecord` inconsistency in `src/acp/store/gateway-events.ts`** (line 50): The helper does not exclude arrays (`!Array.isArray(value)`), unlike the identical helper in `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`. Downstream validators still reject the payload, but with a misleading field-level error rather than "must decode to an object". - The `recordSettledSyntheticFinalCheckpoint` fallback chain in `dispatch-acp-replay.ts` is intentionally complex and is correctly designed for crash recovery. - Error-swallowing in `dispatch-acp.ts` (swallowing `turnError` when the durable run has a "failed" terminal) is intentional and correct [comments/context truncated after 1500 chars] ```

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

Keep the TUI visibly busy while a run is waiting on a follow-up event after `sessions_yield`.

Before this change, the TUI could appear idle even though work was still effectively in flight. The gap showed up when the final assistant message contained the `sessions_yield` follow-up context marker: the run had ended intentionally, but the UI status dropped back to an idle-looking state.

## What changed

- add `awaiting follow-up` as a busy TUI activity state
- detect the `sessions_yield` follow-up marker in final assistant output
- preserve `awaiting follow-up` when reloading session history
- clear stale `awaiting follow-up` state back to `idle` when history no longer contains the marker
- add focused tests for both live event handling and history restore behavior

## Files

- `src/tui/tui.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts`
- `src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts`

## Validation

- direct smoke harness passed for:
  - final event -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - history restore -> `awaiting follow-up`
  - stale follow-up state -> `idle`
- local lint/format checks passed on the touched files

## Notes

A focused local Vitest run hit a Node internal assertion in this environment, so the behavior was additionally checked with a direct harness against the patched TUI logic.
```

github_context:

- Labels: size: L, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 session-state, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, merge-risk: 🚨 availability, status: 📣 needs proof

Body:
```markdown
## Summary
Adds distributed ACP session locking with Redis support and fail-closed behavior when Redis is configured but unavailable.

## What changed
- added `SessionLockManager` abstraction
- added `LocalSessionLockManager`
- added `RedisSessionLockManager`
- added `FailClosedSessionLockManager`
- wired lock acquire/release/renew into `tryDispatchAcpReply`
- skip ACP dispatch with `acp_execution_locked` when lock is contended or acquire fails
- documented:
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_REDIS_URL`
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_TTL_MS`
- added unit + dispatch integration tests

## Locking behavior
- no Redis configured → local in-process lock manager
- Redis configured and healthy → Redis lock manager
- Redis configured but init fails → fail closed, no fallback to local
- release only if owner matches
- renew only if owner matches
- renew is best-effort during long-running turns

## Validation
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm tsgo`
- `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/session-lock-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts`
```

  - `LocalSessionLockManager`
  - `RedisSessionLockManager`
  - `FailClosedSessionLockManager`
- wires lock acquire / renew / release into `tryDispatchAcpReply`
- skips ACP dispatch with `acp_execution_locked` when lock is contended or acquire fails
- documents:
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_REDIS_URL`
  - `OPENCLAW_ACP_SESSION_LOCK_TTL_MS`
- adds unit + dispatch integration coverage

**Behavior:**
- no Redis configured → local in-process lock manager
- Redis configured and healthy → Redis lock manager
- Redis configured but init fails → fail closed, no fallback to local
- Redis release/renew are owner-checked
- renew is best-effort for long-running turns and logs renew failure/error

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #53319: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 53319
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/53319
- Title: [Bug]: ACP concurrent session spawns — first agent fails to launch CC process
- State: OPEN
- Author: bluk1020
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-info, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦐 gold shrimp

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When spawning two ACP sessions (`sessions_spawn runtime:"acp"`) in rapid succession (~37s apart), the first agent's CC process never starts while the second eventually works fine.

## Environment

- OpenClaw `2026.3.22`
- acpx `0.1.16`
- ACP backend: `acpx`, defaultAgent: `claude`
- Node 22.22.1, macOS (arm64)

## Steps to Reproduce

1. From a main session, call `sessions_spawn` twice in quick succession:
   - Agent 1 spawned at `02:22:28 UTC`
   - Agent 2 spawned at `02:23:05 UTC` (37s later)
2. Both return `status: "accepted"` with valid `childSessionKey` and `streamLogPath`

## Observed Behavior

**Agent 1** (`0a73e5e1`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:22:28
- `stall` warning at 02:23:28 (60s, no output)
- **No `assistant_delta` events ever appear** — CC process never produced any output
- `ps aux | grep claude.*reachfar` shows no matching process

**Agent 2** (`ebf86699`):
- `lifecycle:start` emitted at 02:23:05
- `stall` warning at 02:24:05 (60s cold start)
- `assistant_delta` appears at 02:25:17 — CC **starts working normally**
- Progress updates continue ("Now let me read the specific files...")

## Expected Behavior

Both agents should launch successfully. A ~37s gap between spawns should not cause the first to silently fail.

## Stream Log Evidence

**Agent 1 stream log** (3 lines total, never progresses):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → (nothing)
```

**Agent 2 stream log** (resumes after cold start):
```
lifecycle:start → system_event:stall → assistant_delta ("Now let me read...") → system_event:resumed → ...
```

## Workaround

Falling back to direct `claude --print --permission-mode bypassPermissions` via exec works reliably for parallel spawns (tested 3 concurrent agents successfully).

## Analysis

The acpx backend appears to have a race condition during concurrent session initialization. The first session's CC process is either:
1. Never spawned (acpx loses the session init in concurrent handling)
2. Spawned but immediately crashes without emitting any output
3. Blocked waiting on a resource the second session acquires first

Since the session is "accepted" and `lifecycle:start` fires, the issue is downstream of session registration — likely in the acpx CLI launch path.

## Related

- #52878 — ACP backend registration regression
- #53256 — ACP completion relay fix (different bug, addresses result routing)
- #49782 — RFC: ACP completion relay
```

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Regression / runtime bug

### Summary

`sessions_spawn(runtime="subagent")` appears to ignore documented subagent thinking inheritance/defaults and initializes spawned sessions with `thinkingLevel: low`.

On this install:
- parent session thinking is `high`
- per-agent `subagents.thinking` is `high`
- target agent `thinkingDefault` may also be `high`
- but spawned `runtime="subagent"` sessions still begin with `thinking_level_change ... low`

An explicit `sessions_spawn(..., thinking: "high")` **does** work, so the failure appears to be in the inherited/default resolution path, not in the thinking system generally.

### Steps to reproduce

Config excerpt:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "architect",
        "thinkingDefault": "high",
        "subagents": {
          "thinking": "high",
          "allowAgents": ["reviewer", "scribe"]
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "reviewer",
        "model": "github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6",
        "thinkingDefault": "high"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

From a parent agent session whose transcript shows:

```json
{"type":"thinking_level_change", ..., "thinkingLevel":"high"}
```

spawn a child without explicit thinking:

```text
sessions_spawn({
  agentId: "reviewer",
  runtime: "subagent",
  mode: "run",
  task: "Reply with exactly: TEST_OK"
})
```

Then inspect the child session JSONL.

### Expected behavior

The spawned subagent should resolve thinking according to the documented/defaulted chain:
- explicit `sessions_spawn.thinking` if provided
- per-agent `agents.list[].subagents.thinking`
- global `agents.defaults.subagents.thinking`
- otherwise inherit the caller
- then fallback only if none of the above applies

In the repro above, the child should start at `high`, not `low`.

### Actual behavior

The child session starts with:

```json
{"type":"thinking_level_change", ..., "thinkingLevel":"low"}
```

Observed evidence from this install:

Parent architect session:

```json
{"type":"session","version":3,"id":"c58a505a-b235-492a-8959-1af6a03f692a","timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.318Z","cwd":"/home/vrurg"}
{"type":"model_change","id":"01a4ace6","parentId":null,"timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.323Z","provider":"github-copilot","modelId":"gpt-5.4"}
{"type":"thinking_level_change","id":"80963d1f","parentId":"01a4ace6","timestamp":"2026-03-25T15:16:59.323Z","thinkingLevel":"high"}
```

Reviewer child spawned without explicit `thinking`:

```json
{"type":"session","version"

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

1. `agents.list[target].subagents.thinking`
2. `agents.defaults.subagents.thinking`

It does **not** read the target agent's top-level `thinkingDefault`, nor `agents.defaults.thinkingDefault`. Every other thinking-level resolution path in the codebase calls the shared helper `resolveThinkingDefault` (in `dist/model-selection-CTdyYoio.js:11+`) which does honor `thinkingDefault`.

A 108-run benchmark where `sessions_spawn` was intended to orchestrate Pi/Codex runners with `thinkingDefault: "xhigh"` on each target agent. Workaround was to drop `sessions_spawn` entirely and use a CLI subprocess driver (`openclaw agent --json`), because duplicating `subagents.thinking` on every agent plus passing `thinkingLevel` explicitly on every call became fragile.

Call `resolveThinkingDefault` (or inli

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #56613: [Feature]: Talk/Voice tab — agent/session switching + per-agent TTS voice
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 56613
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/56613
- Title: [Feature]: Talk/Voice tab — agent/session switching + per-agent TTS voice
- State: OPEN
- Author: kenchen3000
- Labels: P3, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Two related feature requests for the Android (and iOS/macOS) Talk/Voice mode:

## 1. Voice/Talk tab agent switching

Currently the Voice tab is hardcoded to the `main` session (default agent). The Chat tab already has a session picker that lets users switch between agents, but the Voice tab does not.

**Request:** Allow users to select which agent to talk to in the Voice tab, similar to how the Chat tab session picker works.

**Use case:** Users with multiple agents (e.g., IT Director, Data Scientist, Marketing Director) want to have voice conversations with each of them, not just the default agent.

**Current behavior:** Voice tab always sends to `main` session via `chat.send`. Switching sessions in the Chat tab does not affect the Voice tab.

**Expected behavior:** Voice tab respects the selected session/agent, or has its own agent picker.

**Code reference:** `VoiceTabScreen.kt` has no session selection. `TalkModeManager.kt` uses `mainSessionKey` which defaults to `"main"`. The Chat tab's session switcher (`ChatSheetContent.kt`) already demonstrates the pattern.

## 2. Per-agent TTS voice configuration

Currently TTS voice is a global setting (`messages.tts.microsoft.voice` or `talk.voiceId`). All agents share the same voice.

**Request:** Allow per-agent voice configuration so each agent can have a distinct voice identity.

**Use case:** A user running multiple AI agents wants each agent to sound different — e.g., a deep male voice for one, a warm female voice for another. This makes multi-agent setups more intuitive and immersive.

**Proposed config example:**
```json
{
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-1" }
      },
      {
        "id": "it-director",
        "talk": { "voiceId": "voice-id-2" }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Or via workspace-level config in each agent's workspace files.

### Impact

These two features together would enable natural voice conversations with multiple AI agents, each with their own voice identity — a significant step toward multi-agent voice interaction.

### Environment

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #58411: sessions_spawn lacks --bind here equivalent — agent cannot bind ACP session to existing Discord thread
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 58411
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/58411
- Title: sessions_spawn lacks --bind here equivalent — agent cannot bind ACP session to existing Discord thread
- State: OPEN
- Author: brew-agent
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When a user is in an existing Discord thread and asks the agent to start a coding session, the correct UX is `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here` — which attaches the ACP session to the current thread in place.

However, this slash command can only be run by a human. If the user asks the **agent** to spawn the session, the agent has no programmatic equivalent. `sessions_spawn` only supports `thread: true`, which always **creates a new thread** — which is the wrong behavior here.

## Use case

1. User and agent are having a discussion in an existing Discord thread
2. User says: _"Start a Codex session on this"_
3. Expected: agent binds an ACP session to the current thread — conversation continues in place
4. Actual: agent has to ask the user to manually run `/acp spawn <agent> --bind here`, breaking the flow

## Proposed solution

Add a `bindTo: "current"` option to `sessions_spawn` that maps to `--bind here` semantics — attaches the ACP session to the current thread without creating a new one:

```json
{
  "runtime": "acp",
  "mode": "session",
  "bindTo": "current",
  "task": "..."
}
```

## Related

- #41004 — same slash command vs `sessions_spawn` asymmetry on Telegram
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #59878: Session lane stuck in 'running' after run dies — sessions.abort + gateway restart fail to clear stale state
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 59878
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/59878
- Title: Session lane stuck in 'running' after run dies — sessions.abort + gateway restart fail to clear stale state
- State: OPEN
- Author: Cuttingwater
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug Description

Session lanes get permanently stuck in `status: "running"` after the actual LLM run dies. The stale running state is not cleared by:

1. `sessions.abort` RPC (returns `{"status": "no-active-run"}` confirming the run is dead)
2. Gateway restart (stale state persists through restart)

This blocks the session lane indefinitely — every new message queues behind the dead lock and is never processed.

## Scope

This is broader than #16331 (compaction-triggered lane jams). Any run that dies without proper lifecycle cleanup can cause this — we observed it across webchat, cron, home-node, and default sessions simultaneously.

## Evidence (2026-04-02, version 2026.3.24)

**11 zombie sessions found in a single check**, some stuck for over a week:

```
agent:main:home-tablet-kitchen:  running for 10,571 min (7.3 days)
agent:main:home-tablet-test2:    running for 9,983 min
agent:main:home-tablet-test3:    running for 9,980 min
agent:main:default:              running for 2,888 min (2 days)
agent:main:web_..._7ad797481643: running for 1,309 min
agent:main:cron:721fa3b5-...:    running for 949 min
agent:home:default:              running for 203 min
agent:home:debug:                running for 189 min
```

Every single one returned `"no-active-run"` on `sessions.abort`.

**Gateway logs show lane wait warnings going back hours:**

```
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=1309962 queueAhead=2
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=842773 queueAhead=1
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=539336 queueAhead=0
```

## Impact

- Multiple users unable to get responses on webchat (messages silently queue forever)
- No error surfaced to the user — just permanent silence
- Affects any session type (webchat, cron, home-node, default)

## Workaround

Sending `/new` via `sessions.send` RPC clears the stale state:

```bash
openclaw gateway call sessions.send \
  --params '{"key":"agent:main:main","message":"/new"}' \
  --timeout 15000
```

We have deployed a watchdog script that runs every 10 minutes, detects sessions stuck in "running" beyond 20 minutes with no active run, and auto-recovers via `/new`.

## Expected Behavior

1. `sessions.abort` should clear stale running state when no active run exists
2. Gateway restart should reconcile session status against actual run state
3. Ideally: a session-lane timeout that auto-recovers after a configurable threshold

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.24 (cff6dc

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

github_context:

- Type: issue
- Number: 60737
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60737
- Title: [Feature]: Per-DM/per-chat default ACP binding for auto-spawning ACP sessions on new topics
- State: OPEN
- Author: Lukavyi
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, impact:session-state, impact:security, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
[Feature]: Per-DM/per-chat default ACP binding for auto-spawning ACP sessions on new topics

## Problem

When using Telegram DM forum topics with ACP agents (Claude Code, Codex, etc.), every new topic requires manually running `/acp spawn claude` or asking the main agent to spawn an ACP session. There is no way to configure a DM chat so that new topics automatically bind to an ACP agent.

## Proposed solution

Add a `defaultAcp` config option to `TelegramDirectConfig` (and potentially `TelegramGroupConfig`) that auto-spawns an ACP session when a new topic/thread is created.

### Config example

```json5
{
  "channels": {
    "telegram": {
      "direct": {
        "63448508": {
          "defaultAcp": {
            "agent": "claude",
            "mode": "persistent",
            "cwd": "/workspace/default"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Behavior

1. User creates a new topic in Telegram DM with the bot
2. On first message in the new topic, OpenClaw detects no existing ACP binding
3. OpenClaw auto-spawns an ACP session using `defaultAcp` config
4. The topic is bound to that ACP session
5. All subsequent messages in the topic route directly to the ACP agent
6. Topics with explicit per-topic config (`topics.<id>.agentId`) should override `defaultAcp`

### Scope

- `TelegramDirectConfig.defaultAcp` - for DM topics
- `TelegramGroupConfig.defaultAcp` - for group forum topics
- Could also be a top-level `TelegramAccountConfig.defaultAcp` for account-wide default

### Alternatives considered

- **Static bindings per topic:** Works but requires manual config for each topic ID upfront
- **Main agent with auto-spawn system prompt:** Wastes a main agent turn just to spawn ACP
- **`/acp spawn` in every new topic:** Manual friction, defeats the purpose of topics as lightweight sessions

### Related

- Thread bindings: `threadBindings.spawnAcpSessions`
- Existing ACP binding model: `bindings[].type="acp"`
- Per-topic agentId routing: `topics.<id>.agentId`

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #60979: feature: sessions_spawn ACP delivery to channel (stream output to Zulip/Discord topic)
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 60979
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/60979
- Title: feature: sessions_spawn ACP delivery to channel (stream output to Zulip/Discord topic)
- State: OPEN
- Author: app/spark-of-imagination
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

When spawning an ACP session via `sessions_spawn` (e.g. OpenCode/Codex), there is no way to direct the session's output to a specific channel conversation or topic. This makes it impossible to observe long-running agent tasks in real-time from a chat surface without the parent agent acting as a relay.

## Desired Workflow

```
User ↔ Mimiron (main agent) — discuss task
Mimiron → spawns Jeeves
Jeeves → spawns OpenCode ACP session, output bound to Zulip stream topic
User watches progress live in Zulip topic
Jeeves/Mimiron free to do other things
```

## Current State

- `streamTo: "parent"` exists but only relays events back to the spawning agent (not to a channel)
- `cron` jobs support `delivery.mode: "announce"` which routes output to a channel — no equivalent exists for `sessions_spawn` ACP
- The Zulip channel plugin already implements `ChannelConversationBindingSupport` with `supportsCurrentConversationBinding: true` and a full `SessionBindingAdapter` — the adapter infrastructure is ready

## Proposed Solution

Add a `delivery` option to `sessions_spawn` for `runtime="acp"` sessions, analogous to cron job delivery:

```json
{
  "runtime": "acp",
  "agentId": "opencode",
  "task": "...",
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
    "channel": "zulip",
    "to": "stream:Projects:agent-output"
  }
}
```

This would wire the ACP session output to the specified channel conversation, leveraging the existing `SessionBindingAdapter` infrastructure already present in channel plugins.

## References

- Internal research issue: whizzlelabs/sparkofimagination#23 (options A/B/C analysis)
- Zulip plugin binding adapter: `whizzlelabs/openclaw-zulip` — `src/bindings.ts` — `createZulipSessionBindingAdapter`, `zulipConversationBindingsSupport`
- Cron delivery precedent: `cron` jobs support `delivery.mode: "announce"` for channel output routing

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #64199: [Bug]: ACP configured binding uses parent channel ID for session key — all threads under same channel share one persistent Claude Code process
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 64199
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64199
- Title: [Bug]: ACP configured binding uses parent channel ID for session key — all threads under same channel share one persistent Claude Code process
- State: OPEN
- Author: hoh-dev-bot
- Labels: P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-security-review, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Bug

When an agent uses `runtime.type: \"acp\"` with `mode: \"persistent\"` and a Discord channel binding, **all Discord threads spawned from the same parent channel share a single persistent Claude Code process**. This causes cross-thread context contamination — thread A's agent is aware of thread B's conversation history.

## Root Cause

`buildConfiguredAcpSessionKey` hashes the **configured channel's `peer.id`** (parent channel ID) as `conversationId`, not the individual thread ID:

\`\`\`javascript
// dist/binding-registry-R_XxXX6M.js:26-35
function buildBindingHash(params) {
    return createHash("sha256")
        .update(\`\${params.channel}:\${params.accountId}:\${params.conversationId}\`)
        .digest("hex").slice(0, 16);
}
function buildConfiguredAcpSessionKey(spec) {
    const hash = buildBindingHash({ channel, accountId, conversationId });
    return \`agent:\${agentId}:acp:binding:\${channel}:\${accountId}:\${hash}\`;
}
\`\`\`

Since `conversationId` = the configured `peer.id` (parent channel), every thread from that channel resolves to the **same session key** → same `acpxRecordId` → same entry in `AcpRuntimeManager.pendingPersistentClients` → **same Claude Code process**.

Verified by hashing sima's two configured Discord parent channels:

\`\`\`python
import hashlib
hashlib.sha256("discord:default:1471512512175407297".encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
# → "17afdbb6be5eba6c"  ✅ matches actual acpx state record on disk

hashlib.sha256("discord:default:1475705305584373780".encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
# → "a709dafc3a7c44dc"  ✅ matches actual acpx state record on disk
\`\`\`

Despite 20+ Discord threads each having their own entry in `sessions.json` (`agent:sima:discord:channel:<threadId>`), the acpx state directory only contains **2 sima binding records** — one per parent channel, not one per thread.

## Contrast with CLI backend

CLI and native backends use `buildAgentPeerSessionKey` which correctly uses the thread ID as `peerId`:

\`\`\`javascript
// session-key-BMb3Kc4r.js:173
return \`agent:\${agentId}:\${channel}:\${peerKind}:\${peerId}\`;
// → "agent:sima:discord:channel:<threadId>"  — per thread ✅
\`\`\`

Only `runtime.type: "acp"` with a configured channel binding is affected.

## Layer comparison

| Layer | Key format | Granularity |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| OpenClaw session store (sessions.json) | `agent:sima:discord:channel:<threadId>` | per thread ✅ |
| ACP binding session (acpx state) | `agent:sima:acp:binding:discord:def

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

- State: OPEN
- Author: dougvk
- Labels: bug, bug:crash, P1, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:crash-loop, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Bug type

Crash (process/app exits or hangs)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

On Omarchy/Hyprland/Wayland, a managed headed Chromium tab can load successfully but the first normal viewport screenshot still times out on some pages.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start OpenClaw `2026.4.8` from a source checkout on Omarchy/Hyprland/Wayland with `browser.headless=false`.
2. Launch a fresh managed headed Chromium session.
3. Navigate to `https://github.com/trending` or `https://finance.yahoo.com/`.
4. Request the first normal viewport screenshot for the loaded page.
5. Observe a screenshot timeout even though the page is already open.

### Expected behavior

A page that has already loaded successfully in the managed headed browser should also return a normal viewport screenshot; this was observed on the same machine with fresh headless Chromium and with a Hyprland fake-output + `grim` workaround.

### Actual behavior

On fresh headed Wayland sessions, the first viewport screenshot timed out at about `13s` on GitHub Trending and Yahoo Finance with `Page.captureScreenshot timed out after 10000ms`, even though the page had already loaded.

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.8

### Operating system

Arch Linux (Omarchy), Hyprland / Wayland

### Install method

Source checkout, built locally, launched as a user systemd service running `dist/index.js gateway`

### Model

openai-codex/gpt-5.4

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> openai-codex

### Additional provider/model setup details

Current local config sets `agents.defaults.model.primary` to `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`. The observed failure is in browser screenshot capture, not in model generation.

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

- Fresh headed Wayland first-shot results:
  - `https://github.com/trending`: timeout at about `13017ms`, error `Page.captureScreenshot timed out after 10000ms`
  - `https://finance.yahoo.com/`: timeout at about `13008ms`, error `Page.captureScreenshot timed out after 10000ms`
- Same-machine controls in the same investigation:
  - `youtube.com`: first screenshot typically about `2.0s`
  - `nytimes.com`: first screenshot about `1.3s` to `1.7s`
- Earlier Fastmail session evidence showed the page opened first and the browser process remained alive after screenshot timeout, so the later `browser unavailable` message was a misclassification rather than the initial failure.
- Local workaround evidence:
  - native `grim` capture on a dedicated Hyprland fake output: about `20ms` to 

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

Comments/context:
```markdown
- Angfr95 at 2026-04-15T11:30:47Z:
Fix submitted in the PR linked above.

The CDP `Page.captureScreenshot` path stalls on headed Hyprland/Wayland for certain pages. The fix adds a native fallback: detect the Hyprland session, create a dedicated headless output, move the browser window onto it, and capture with `grim` (~20-170ms). Falls back to CDP transparently when not on Hyprland or for full-page/element screenshots.

Based on the workaround from https://gist.github.com/dougvk/e0fb18de87b11a9f58cf991ed6e95618.

When no explicit exec overrides are configured and the sender is not an owner (`senderIsOwner === false`), the function returns `{ security: "deny", ask: "always" }` instead of `undefined` (which would fall through to the permissive global defaults). - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Owner sessions and explicitly configured agent/session/directive overrides are unaffected. github_context:
GitHub item: - Repository: openclaw/openclaw - Type: pull_request - Number: 66327 - URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66327 - Title: feat(msteams): implement sendPayload for interactive approval cards - State: OPEN - Author: johnturek - Labels: channel: msteams, size: M, triage: needs-real-behavior-proof, P2, rating: 🧂 unranked krab, merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery, status: 📣 needs proof - Changed file count available to wrapper: 3 - Changed files: extensions/msteams/src/channel.ts, extensions/msteams/src/outbound.test.ts, extensions/msteams/src/outbound.ts Body: ```markdown ## Summary Implements `sendPayload` for the MS Teams channel extension so approval prompts render as Adaptive Cards with clickable buttons instead of plain text with raw `/approve` commands. Closes #64690 ## Problem The Teams outbound adapter only implemented `sendText`, `sendMedia`, and `sendPoll`. The delivery pipeline checks `handler.sendPayload && hasReplyPayloadContent(...)` before dispatching interactive payloads — since Teams had no `sendPayload`, all approval prompts fell through to `sendText`, rendering as: > Reply /approve abc123 yes to approve ## Solution Added `sendPayload` to the Teams outbound adapter following the patterns established by Slack, Discord, and Telegram: 1. **`extensions/msteams/src/outbound.ts`**: - Added `buildApprovalAdaptiveCard()` helper that maps interactive button blocks to Adaptive Card `Action.Submit` buttons - Uses `messageBack` (not `imBack`) to avoid echoing raw approval tokens — matches the existing poll card pattern in `polls.ts` - Honors pre-built `channelData.msteams.card` if supplied - Uses `resolveInteractiveTextFallback()` to derive card body text from text blocks - Sends media first via `sendPayloadMediaSequenceAndFinalize`, then finalizes with the card - Falls back to `sendTextMediaPayload` when no interactive buttons are present 2. **`extensions/msteams/src/channel.ts`**: - Wired `sendPayload` directly on the outbound config (since `createRuntimeOutboundDelegates` only supports sendText/sendMedia/sendPoll), following the Slack channel pattern Result: Approval prompts render as Adaptive Cards with "Approve" / "Deny" buttons. Clicking a button sends the `/approve` command via messageBack. ## Testing - 3 new test cases in `outbound.test.ts`: - Interactive buttons → Adaptive Card with messageBack actions - No buttons → falls back to sendText - Pre-built channelData card → sent directly - All 66 Teams extension test files pass (860 tests) - Run: `pnpm test:extension msteams` ## AI Disclosure - [x] AI-assisted (GitHub Copilot CLI) - [x] Fully tested — `pnpm test:extension msteams` passes (860/860 tests) - [x] I understand what the code does - [x] I work at Microsoft and can verify this against a real Teams tenant ``` Comments/context: ```markdown - greptile-apps at 2026-04-14T04:04:41Z:

github_context:

- State: OPEN
- Author: sazora
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, impact:message-loss, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
OpenClaw explicit `agent --json` ACP runs currently hit two separate bugs in the embedded explicit-run path. Both are fixed locally for now, but only as hotfixes in installed Homebrew `dist` files.

## Environment

- OpenClaw installed via Homebrew
- Patched runtime files currently under `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/`
- Repro workspace: `/Users/stevebot/.openclaw/workspace`
- Repro target path: `/Volumes/4TB-SSD/Projects/wrap-business`

## Repro command

```bash
openclaw agent \
  --session-id 'agent:main:discord:channel:1493603713426788494' \
  --message 'Use Codex via ACP. Create exactly one file at /Volumes/4TB-SSD/Projects/wrap-business/oc-acp-write-test-smoke.txt containing exactly: ACP SSD smoke test ok.' \
  --json \
  --timeout 180
```

Optional verification:

```bash
cat /Volumes/4TB-SSD/Projects/wrap-business/oc-acp-write-test-smoke.txt
```

## Expected behavior

- The explicit ACP run should succeed when the embedded ACP backend is healthy.
- The target file should be created with the requested contents.
- Final JSON should reflect the completed state, including:
  - `status: "ok"`
  - `finalAssistantVisibleText`
  - `replayInvalid: false`
  - `livenessState: "completed"`

## Actual behavior

Two different failures were observed:

1. **Embedded explicit ACP backend visibility failure**
   - Explicit `agent --json` ACP runs could fail with:
     - `ACP runtime backend is not configured. Install and enable the acpx runtime plugin.`
   - At the same time, gateway logs showed the backend was already healthy, e.g.:
     - `embedded acpx runtime backend ready`

2. **Stale final JSON state after `sessions_yield`**
   - Even when the ACP descendant work completed successfully and the file existed on disk, explicit `agent --json` could still return stale metadata such as:
     - `replayInvalid: true`
     - `livenessState: "working"`

## Root cause summary

### Bug 1: explicit embedded process could not see ACP backend
ACP backend registration was process-local. The gateway process had registered the embedded ACPX backend, but the explicit embedded run process performed backend lookup without ensuring local registration first.

### Bug 2: explicit final-state reconciliation depended on initial assistant text
In the explicit agent RPC path, descendant completion reconciliation after `sessions_yield` was gated on `initialAssistantText`. If the parent yielded before producing assistant-visible text, reconciliation was skipped, leaving stale

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #68187: SSE-backed MCP sessions can stay stale after server restart and fail with 'Session not found'
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 68187
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/68187
- Title: SSE-backed MCP sessions can stay stale after server restart and fail with 'Session not found'
- State: OPEN
- Author: gwjr
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

After an SSE-backed MCP server restarts, existing OpenClaw client/proxy sessions can keep using stale session state and fail with `Session not found` instead of reconnecting cleanly.

## Environment

- OpenClaw CLI/app: `2026.4.14`
- Host: macOS (Darwin arm64)
- Local MCP services managed under `~/Developer/mcp-deployment`
- Observed while using local MCP-backed tools behind the OpenClaw gateway/proxy layer

## Observed behaviour

When an SSE-backed MCP service is restarted, the next tool call from an already-established OpenClaw-side client session can fail with stale session state rather than transparently recovering.

The concrete failure seen locally was `Session not found` immediately after server restart, with recovery requiring a full OpenClaw gateway restart to force fresh sessions.

## Expected behaviour

If the downstream SSE-backed MCP server restarts, the OpenClaw client/proxy layer should detect the dead/stale session, reconnect, and establish a fresh session automatically, or at least invalidate and recreate the client session on the next call instead of pinning the caller to a dead one.

## Repro shape

1. Start OpenClaw with an MCP integration that talks through an SSE-backed server.
2. Make a successful tool call so the client/proxy session is established.
3. Restart the SSE-backed MCP server.
4. Make another tool call through the same OpenClaw path.
5. Observe a stale-session failure (`Session not found`) until the OpenClaw gateway itself is restarted.

## Current workaround

Restart the OpenClaw gateway to force new client sessions.

## Notes

This was originally tracked locally as an upstream-only defect because the failure appears to live in the OpenClaw gateway/proxy client-session layer, not in the downstream MCP service repos.

```

github_context:

- State: OPEN

- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🌊 off-meta tidepool

Body:
```markdown
**Labels:** `architecture`, `observability`, `tracing`, `P0`

### Summary
Create one canonical run trace schema that works across main agent runs, ACP sessions, subagents, and task flows.

### Why
Tracing exists, but it is fragmented and too low-level. When a run goes wrong, it is hard to quickly answer:
- what happened
- what step failed
- what tools were involved
- whether delegation happened
- how parent and child runs relate

### Scope
Define a schema with fields like:
- `runId`
- `sessionKey`
- `parentRunId`
- `taskId`
- `stepId`
- `eventType`
- `eventSource`
- `toolName`
- `handoffType`
- `status`
- `startedAt`
- `endedAt`
- `latencyMs`
- `summary`

Adopt it in:
- core agent tracing
- ACP parent-child relay paths
- task execution progress and terminal events

### Likely files
- `src/agents/trace-base.ts`
- `src/agents/cache-trace.ts`
- `src/agents/acp-spawn-parent-stream.ts`
- `src/tasks/task-executor.ts`
- new: `src/agents/run-trace.ts`

### Acceptance criteria
- one canonical trace schema exists
- main, ACP, and subagent/task runs emit into it
- parent-child linkage is preserved
- step-level and tool-level events are supported
- trace data can reconstruct a compact run timeline

### Checklist
- [ ] define schema types
- [ ] add parent-child linkage fields
- [ ] map current trace producers to schema
- [ ] update ACP relay path
- [ ] update task executor paths
- [ ] add tests

---

```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #71157: [Feature]: Support WhatsApp disappearing-message expiration for outbound replies
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 71157
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/71157
- Title: [Feature]: Support WhatsApp disappearing-message expiration for outbound replies
- State: OPEN
- Author: dougvk
- Labels: enhancement, P2, clawsweeper:fix-shape-clear, clawsweeper:queueable-fix, clawsweeper:source-repro, impact:security, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
### Summary

Allow WhatsApp channel/account config to apply a default disappearing-message expiration to outbound OpenClaw replies.

### Problem to solve

WhatsApp supports disappearing messages, but OpenClaw’s WhatsApp outbound sends do not currently expose a reliable way to apply an expiration to agent replies. In chats where disappearing messages are expected, OpenClaw replies can persist longer than the surrounding conversation policy, creating a privacy mismatch and requiring manual cleanup.

### Proposed solution

Add WhatsApp-specific config for outbound disappearing-message expiration:

  channels.whatsapp.disappearingMessagesSeconds?: number
  channels.whatsapp.accounts.<accountId>.disappearingMessagesSeconds?: number

  Behavior:

  - Account-level setting overrides channel-level setting.
  - Positive values are passed to WhatsApp/Baileys outbound sends as ephemeralExpiration.
  - 0, null, or omitted means no explicit OpenClaw override.
  - If no config override exists, optionally honor Baileys credential default disappearing mode when available.
  - Apply consistently across WhatsApp outbound send paths: normal text, quoted replies, polls, media, and tool/channel sends.
  - Keep existing behavior unchanged unless configured or a WhatsApp account default is available.

### Alternatives considered

Manual deletion or prompt-level instructions are unreliable because the expiration is transport metadata, not message text. Relying only on WhatsApp UI chat settings is also insufficient if the outbound API path does not propagate the expiration consistently.

### Impact

Affected: WhatsApp channel users, especially private/family/team chats using disappearing-message norms.

  Severity: Medium. It does not block all WhatsApp use, but it creates a privacy and policy mismatch.

  Frequency: Every outbound OpenClaw WhatsApp reply in disappearing-message contexts.

  Consequence: Agent replies can persist unexpectedly, causing extra manual cleanup and reducing trust in using OpenClaw in privacy-sensitive WhatsApp chats.

### Evidence/examples

Baileys supports passing ephemeralExpiration via message generation/send options. A local fork patch confirmed the shape works by applying configured expiration to outbound WhatsApp sends.

### Additional information

This should be backward-compatible and should include config schema/docs updates plus tests for channel-level config, account-level override, unset behavior, and quoted/media send paths.
```

Body:
```markdown
# Proposed upstream issue — openclaw/openclaw

**Title:** Config schema: add `sandbox`, `routing.rules`, `instances`, and `gateway.nodes.denyPaths`

---

## Context

Running OpenClaw v2026.4.22 on macOS (Mac Studio M4 Max) as a single-user local gateway at `127.0.0.1:18789`. Using it for a mixed cloud / local-Ollama setup with sensitivity-based routing.

The current schema covers auth, bind, tailscale, controlUi, `gateway.nodes.denyCommands`, and `session.dmScope`. Four adjacent capabilities are not in the schema and would meaningfully reduce wrapper / sidecar code.

## Asks

1. **`sandbox.mode`** — global sandbox mode for tool execution. At minimum two values: `main` (current behavior) and `non-main` (sensitive operations refuse outside an approval flow). Today `appServer.mode: guardian` exists but is Codex-plugin-scoped.

2. **`routing.rules`** — tag-based provider selection. Useful shape:
   ```json
   {
     "routing": {
       "defaults": { "provider": "anthropic" },
       "rules": [
         {
           "match": { "tags": ["sensitive", "personal-finance"] },
           "provider": "ollama",
           "host": "127.0.0.1:11434",
           "model": "llama3:70b"
         }
       ]
     }
   }
   ```
   Any skill/agent call carrying one of the tags routes to the matching provider. Today this has to be enforced by a skill-level library that reads a policy sidecar.

3. **`instances.*`** — multi-instance awareness in one config so multiple gateways on the same host (one per user) can be declared and validated together, with per-instance workspace + port + active/deferred state. Today each instance needs a separate config file and wrapper-enforced isolation.

4. **`gateway.nodes.denyPaths`** — symmetric with the existing `denyCommands` array. A list of absolute paths/prefixes the gateway refuses to read or write regardless of which tool is asking. Covers credentials dirs, SSH keys, secrets archives.

## Why it matters

Without these, the policy gets split across the config, a sidecar JSON file, and per-skill code. Any skill that forgets the guard silently falls back to the default provider or touches a denyPath. Moving the four capabilities into the gateway would let `openclaw doctor` validate them and the gateway enforce them at one chokepoint.

## Happy to help

Willing to draft schema PRs for (1) and (4) if the project agrees the shape. (2) and (3) have more design surface and probably benefit from maintainer direction first.

---

**Environment**

- 

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

target: openclaw/openclaw github_issue #71646: mcp/channel-bridge: pendingClaudePermissions / pendingApprovals leak — no TTL, no close-clear, no cap
github_context:
GitHub item:
- Repository: openclaw/openclaw
- Type: issue
- Number: 71646
- URL: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/71646
- Title: mcp/channel-bridge: pendingClaudePermissions / pendingApprovals leak — no TTL, no close-clear, no cap
- State: OPEN
- Author: Feelw00
- Labels: P2, clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr, clawsweeper:needs-product-decision, clawsweeper:source-repro, clawsweeper:linked-pr-open, impact:session-state, issue-rating: 🦞 diamond lobster

Body:
```markdown
## Summary

`OpenClawChannelBridge` (`src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts`) holds two instance-bound pending Maps that lack the same three guards their siblings already have, so a long-running `openclaw mcp serve` process accumulates entries monotonically.

| Collection | Cap | Close-clear | TTL / sweeper |
|---|---|---|---|
| `queue` (Array, L48) | `QUEUE_LIMIT=1000` while-shift (L355) | n/a | n/a |
| `pendingWaiters` (Set, L49) | n/a | `close()` clears (L148) | per-waiter `setTimeout` fallback (L265) |
| `pendingClaudePermissions` (Map, L50) | **none** | **none** | **none** |
| `pendingApprovals` (Map, L51) | **none** | **none** | `expiresAtMs` is stored at L382 but never drives a timer/sweeper |

## Why it matters

- **`pendingClaudePermissions`**: Claude SDK sends a `notifications/claude/channel/permission_request` for every tool call (`channel-server.ts:42-49`). The entry is only removed when the operator replies with `yes <id>` / `no <id>` matching `/^(yes|no)\s+([a-km-z]{5})$/i` (`channel-bridge.ts:455-459`). Missed/typo'd/cross-channel responses leave the entry forever. `requestId` is fresh per request so there is no overwrite.
- **`pendingApprovals`**: `trackApproval` (L369-) sets the entry on `exec.approval.requested` / `plugin.approval.requested` and only deletes on the matching `*.resolved` event (L389). A gateway WebSocket drop (`channel-bridge.ts:122-124` `onClose` is reject-only) silently loses the resolved frame and the entry persists.
- `close()` (L136-152) clears `pendingWaiters` but does not clear either pending Map.

In a long-running `openclaw mcp serve` process (operator hours-units, `claudeChannelMode != 'off'` busy automation), both Maps grow monotonically.

## Affected paths

- `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts:50` (`pendingClaudePermissions` decl)
- `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts:51` (`pendingApprovals` decl)
- `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts:136-152` (`close()` body — pending Map clears missing)
- `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts:273-295` (`handleClaudePermissionRequest` set without TTL)
- `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts:369-391` (`trackApproval` stores `expiresAtMs` but no expiry mechanism)

## Severity

P2 — slow memory-growth leak in opt-in long-running CLI path. Not an immediate OOM, but unbounded over time. Maintainer-priority axes hit: **memory + reliability + plugin loading (MCP)**.

Body:
```markdown
# Bug: Memory search transient `fetch failed | other side closed` / `Client network socket disconnected before secure TLS connection was established` for live embedding queries (provider-agnostic)

## Summary

Live memory search queries fail intermittently (~20–40% of calls) with one of two transient TLS/socket errors when using **any** remote embedding provider (OpenAI, Gemini). The same endpoint works perfectly via `curl` and via a plain Node.js `fetch()` from the same host, so the upstream API is healthy. The failure originates inside OpenClaw's internal SSRF-guarded fetch path.

Bulk reindex via the batch endpoint is **not** affected. Only the per-query single-embed path used by `openclaw memory search` (and presumably the in-conversation memory recall path) shows the issue.

This makes semantic memory recall unreliable in interactive sessions even though `openclaw memory status` reports `Embeddings: ready`.

---

## Environment

| Item | Value |
| --- | --- |
| OpenClaw version | `2026.4.24 (cbcfdf6)` |
| Node.js | `v24.14.1` |
| OS | Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, kernel `6.8.0-110-generic` (x86_64) |
| Network | direct outbound, no proxy, IPv4+IPv6 both working |
| `memory.backend` | `builtin` |
| sqlite-vec | enabled, `vec0.so` loaded, `Vector dims: 3072`, `FTS: ready` |

Reproduced on two different remote embedding providers configured via `openclaw config set`:

- `openai` / `text-embedding-3-large` (3072-dim, ~30 KB response)
- `gemini` / `gemini-embedding-2-preview` (3072-dim, ~60 KB response)

Both fail with the same intermittent socket error. The Gemini case fails more often, consistent with a payload-size correlation, but OpenAI also fails repeatably.

---

## Repro

### 1. Configure a remote embedding provider

```bash
openclaw config set memory.backend builtin
openclaw config set agents.defaults.memorySearch.provider openai
openclaw config set agents.defaults.memorySearch.model text-embedding-3-large
openclaw config set models.providers.openai \
  '{"baseUrl":"https://api.openai.com/v1","apiKey":"sk-...","models":[]}' --strict-json
openclaw gateway restart
```

### 2. Reindex (works fine, uses batch endpoint)

```bash
openclaw memory index --force --agent main
# → Memory index updated (main).
```

`openclaw memory status --deep --agent main` then reports:

```
Provider: openai (requested: openai)
Model: text-embedding-3-large
Vector: ready
Vector dims: 3072
FTS: ready
Embeddings: ready
```

### 3. Run live queries (fails ~20–40% of the time)

```bash
f

[body truncated after 2500 chars]
```

github_context:
